recording_notes: Direct camera inside blue FLS trainer — second attempt on this third equipment configuration. ~146s clip, high visual quality. Hand-lead reads remain difficult from the top-down view but knot structure and drain outcome are clearly assessable.

frame_analyses.description: First instrument becomes visible and the task begins.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Timing starts here; entry is prompt and controlled.

frame_analyses.description: The needle is loaded and being aligned toward the Penrose targets for the initial bite.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Needle orientation is acceptable, though there is some setup/regrasping before the pass.

frame_analyses.description: The needle has traversed both sides of the slit and the suture is being pulled through the marked area.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Placement appears close to the target dots, but deviation measurement is low-confidence from this camera angle.

frame_analyses.description: The first throw is being formed and cinched down at the Penrose.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Two wraps appear visible before the tail is drawn through, consistent with a surgeon's knot; left-hand lead is the best estimate.

frame_analyses.description: The second throw is being laid down to reverse the first throw and square the knot.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Single-throw configuration appears correct; hand switch is likely present, though this is only moderate-confidence from the top-down view.

frame_analyses.description: The final throw is being formed and tightened to secure the knot.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: The knot is squared with controlled tension, but multiple regrasping motions slow this phase.

frame_analyses.description: The knot is seated and the suture tails are being presented for intracorporeal cutting.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: No avulsion is seen, and there is no obvious slit gap at the knot site.

frame_analyses.description: Both tails have just been divided and the instruments begin to withdraw.

knot_assessments.notes: Best estimate is a left-led double throw; two wraps appear visible before cinching, consistent with a surgeon's knot.
Single throw appears to reverse the first throw and lie down appropriately; right-hand lead is the best estimate, with only moderate confidence.
Final single throw appears to alternate back and square the knot; left-hand lead is the best estimate.

suture_placement.placement_notes: Bites appear close to the marked dots but precise mm measurement is not possible from the top-down direct camera angle.

drain_assessment.assessment_note: No visible gap at the knot site, drain not avulsed, knot stack remains seated through cut.

technique_summary: The stitch placement appears close to the marked targets and the drain remains securely attached throughout the task. The final knot appears seated without an obvious slit gap or gross slip, but the run is slowed by repeated regrasping/reset motions during knot tying and a relatively long tail-management phase. Hand alternation appears to have been attempted on throws 2 and 3, although that is the lowest-confidence element of this assessment from the top-down view. This is a strong pass-level second attempt on the blue trainer.
improvement_suggestions: Reduce the reset/regrasp time after the stitch is passed so the first throw can begin sooner.
Make the hand switch between throws more deliberate and visually obvious to avoid any risk of a switching penalty.
Present both tails for cutting earlier after the third throw to shorten the final intracorporeal cut phase.
strengths: Needle passage appears close to both target marks and the Penrose was not avulsed during the task.
The completed knot appears to seat down adequately with good slit approximation and no obvious loosening.
Second consecutive direct-camera run with high GPT-4o confidence (0.69), confirming rapid blue-trainer adaptation.
comparison_notes: Effectively exact score agreement with Claude V31 at delta 0.1 FLS (457.9 vs 458.0) — the tightest agreement in the entire series. Both teachers see the same competent-but-inefficient run with secure final knot and intact drain. Claude penalties (5.0) are 2.5 above GPT-4o's rubric-strict 2.5 — seventh case in the out-of-rubric quality/efficiency pattern (V20, V22, V26, V28, V29, V30, V31). Phase split disagreement is the same INVERSE pattern as V30: GPT-4o keeps cut short (16.7s) while Claude bundles more into cut (47s); the difference goes back into placement (35 vs 18) and throws on the GPT-4o side. Tails converge within 2.6s. Both teachers agree V31 is a 13% improvement over V30 (137-139.6s vs 158-161.8s) on the same equipment, validating rapid blue-trainer adaptation.
